text: taxify auto-downloads backbones first use. call taxify(names, backend = "wfo") local WFO backbone exists, taxify fetches pre-built .vtr file Zenodo, writes taxify_data_dir(), caches path remainder R session. Subsequent calls, whether session future sessions, reuse local copy without network access. download backbone ahead time (useful shared server Docker image), use taxify_download_vtr(): Pre-built .vtr files hosted Zenodo typically range 50 MB (AlgaeBase) 400 MB (GBIF), depending backend. files compiled raw Darwin Core sources precomputed matching keys, embedded synonym resolution, genus-level indexes, ready querying moment download completes. taxify checks backbone updates per R session. first taxify() call session fetches manifest GitHub, compares requested backend’s local version latest available release, downloads new version one exists. network unavailable, taxify falls back bundled manifest uses whatever local copy disk. version check logged console silent updates. backends large source files, build--source path also exists. taxify_download("gbif") downloads raw 1.5 GB simple.txt.gz GBIF, parses 30 positional columns, denormalizes family hierarchy via self-joins, compiles result .vtr format. slower downloading pre-built file produces output. build--source path mainly useful CI pipelines users want customize compilation step. can also pin specific backbone version: Pinned versions stored directory (taxify_data_dir()/wfo/2024.01/) never overwritten future updates. “latest” slot (taxify_data_dir()/wfo/latest/) always overwritten newer version becomes available. title: Choosing and combining backends
text: simplest use case: match plant names WFO. Every row output 16 columns regardless backend produced : input_name, matched_name, accepted_name, taxon_id, accepted_id, rank, family, genus, epithet, authorship, is_synonym, is_hybrid, match_type, fuzzy_dist, backend, backbone_version. backend column records "wfo" matched rows NA unmatched ones. backbone_version column records backend name, version, download date (e.g., "wfo:2024-12 (2026-04-01)") can cite exact data snapshot used. name matches synonym, taxify automatically resolves accepted name. matched_name column shows name string actually matched backbone (may synonym), accepted_name shows current accepted name resolution. is_synonym column TRUE resolved synonyms, FALSE direct matches. Fuzzy matching default normalized Damerau-Levenshtein threshold 0.2, roughly one edit per five characters. catches common typos like transposed letters missing diacritics. can tighten threshold stricter matching: disable fuzzy matching entirely accept exact case-insensitive matches: Two alternative distance metrics available via fuzzy_method argument: "levenshtein" (standard Levenshtein, transposition handling) "jw" (Jaro-Winkler, better names differ mainly beginnings). default "dl" (Damerau-Levenshtein) good -rounder.

title: Choosing and combining backends
text: right backend depends taxonomic scope data. decision tree organized common use cases. Pure vascular plant lists. Use backend = "wfo". WFO standard reference, well-curated, updated regularly. names fall (e.g., horticultural cultivars, nomenclaturally complex genera, names older floras use outdated synonymy), add COL: backend = c("wfo", "col"). Pure marine/aquatic lists. Use backend = "worms". WoRMS authoritative source marine taxonomy, curated domain experts, includes habitat extinction flags. estuarine transitional lists include terrestrial taxa, add COL: backend = c("worms", "col"). Pure fungal lists. Use backend = "fungorum". Species Fungorum Plus specialist reference fungi. Add COL fallback obscure recently described species: backend = c("fungorum", "col"). Pure algal lists. Use backend = "algaebase". Add COL WoRMS fallback: backend = c("algaebase", "col"). Remember AlgaeBase CC -NC. Mixed-kingdom ecological datasets. Use backend = c("col", "gbif"), lead specialist backend dominant taxon group. plant-dominated dataset animals fungi: backend = c("wfo", "col"). marine biodiversity survey: backend = c("worms", "col", "gbif"). forest inventory includes trees, fungi, insects, epiphytes: backend = c("wfo", "fungorum", "col"). Molecular/sequence-linked work. Use backend = "ncbi". NCBI Taxonomy reference GenBank, BOLD, sequence databases. covers bacteria, archaea, viruses backends lack. mixed molecular ecological work: backend = c("ncbi", "col"). Phylogenetic studies. Use backend = "ott". OTT backbone Open Tree Life merges multiple source taxonomies. cross-references NCBI, GBIF, WoRMS, IRMNG make good bridge different identifier systems. Maximum coverage / catch-. Use backend = c("col", "gbif"). COL provides expert-curated taxonomy ~4.5M names. GBIF’s backbone adds ~10M names additional sources. Together cover virtually described species nomenclatural record. combination reasonable default taxonomic composition dataset unknown. General rule. Specialist backends first, generalist backends second. Lead backend whose taxonomic opinion trust dominant taxon group, add broader backends fallbacks remainder. backend column output lets us audit exactly taxonomic opinion applied name.

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: Taxonomic name matching rarely last step. taxify() resolves species list accepted names IDs, next task usually attach trait data, occurrence records, measurement tables external sources. trouble external datasets almost never use names backbone. CSV leaf trait measurements might record Pinus nigra subsp. laricio, backbone stores accepted name Pinus nigra. colleague’s spreadsheet might list Picea excelsa (synonym retired decades ago), WFO recognises Picea abies. Joining raw species strings fails silently cases: rows simply match, merged data.frame full NAs values exist. standard workaround run external names backbone first, resolve accepted IDs, join IDs. add_data() wraps entire workflow single pipe step.
code: library(taxify)

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: common case: trait measurements data.frame sitting R session, want attach taxify() result. create small table specific leaf area (SLA) maximum height five European tree species. add_data() takes names taxon column, runs backbone(s) used original taxify() call, resolves accepted_id, left-joins ID. Picea excelsa synonym Picea abies WFO, SLA height values land correct row even though literal strings differ. output two new columns, sla max_height_m, appended existing result.
code: # Our taxify result species <- c(   "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea abies",   "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula" ) result <- taxify(species, backend = "wfo")  # External trait data -- note one synonym and one subspecies traits <- data.frame(   taxon = c(     "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea excelsa",     "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula"   ),   sla = c(18.2, 24.1, 6.5, 8.0, 22.3),   max_height_m = c(35, 40, 50, 30, 25) )  # Join -- "Picea excelsa" resolves to "Picea abies" through the backbone result <- result |> add_data(traits, species_col = "taxon")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: data lives file rather memory, can pass path directly. add_data() reads .csv .csv.gz files via vectra’s CSV reader, handles large files efficiently without loading everything R . CSV single obvious species-name column, auto-detection picks . several plausible character columns, names encoded column unusual name like latin_binomial, specifying species_col avoids ambiguity. pattern works compressed CSV files. .csv.gz path detected decompressed transparently.
code: result <- taxify(species, backend = "wfo") result <- result |> add_data("path/to/leaf_traits.csv") result |> add_data("leaf_traits.csv", species_col = "latin_binomial") result |> add_data("global_leaf_traits.csv.gz", species_col = "species")

title: Joining custom data with add_data()
text: External datasets sometimes contain species . happens repeated measurements across sites, multiple literature sources compiled one table, subspecies resolve accepted species. add_data() distinguishes two cases. Exact duplicates occur trait values given species identical across repeated rows. harmless: duplicates collapsed single row warning. Conflicting duplicates occur species appears different trait values. “Conflicting” means least one selected trait columns differs two rows share accepted_id. comparison column--column treats two NA values equal (missing counts agreement). two rows Quercus robur SLA values 18.2 21.5, conflict. rows SLA 18.2 one NA wood density 0.56, also conflict. every selected column matches exactly across rows given species consider duplicates identical. add_data() decide value correct conflict exists, raises error names offending species. fix depends data. duplicates represent within-species variation (e.g., measurements different populations), aggregating joining right approach. represent data entry errors, removing bad rows resolves issue. cols argument can also help columns conflict: selecting non-conflicting subset lets join proceed. Note duplicates checked backbone resolution, raw names. external data lists Picea excelsa Picea abies different SLA values, two names resolve accepted species trigger conflicting-duplicate error. intentional: join key accepted ID, conflicting values key coexist.
code: # Harmless: same species, same values (perhaps from two sites) dup_ok <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   sla = c(18.2, 18.2, 24.1) ) result |> add_data(dup_ok, species_col = "species") # Warning: 1 duplicate rows ... deduplicated. # Conflicting: same species, different SLA values dup_bad <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica"),   sla = c(18.2, 21.5, 24.1) ) result |> add_data(dup_bad, species_col = "species") # Error: 1 species resolved to the same accepted_id but have #   different trait values. #   Examples: 'Quercus robur' (wfo-0000309171) # Aggregate first, then join library(stats) dup_agg <- aggregate(sla ~ species, data = dup_bad, FUN = mean) result |> add_data(dup_agg, species_col = "species")

Columns containing site codes, habitat labels, observer names rarely match backbone entry, true species column tends stand clearly. Match species names backbone(s) used original taxify() call. produces accepted_id row external data. backbone choice read taxify_meta attribute taxify() attaches output, need specify . Fuzzy matching default (controlled via fuzzy fuzzy_threshold). names fail resolve dropped joinable pool, count appears summary message end. Check duplicates. backbone resolution, rows share accepted_id inspected. Exact duplicates (identical trait values across selected columns) collapsed warning. Conflicting duplicates raise error, described section . Left join accepted_id. Every row original taxify() result matching accepted_id external data receives trait columns. Rows without match get NA. Column name collisions resolved prefixing incoming columns data_. join preserves every row original result; nothing dropped. what: EIVE ecological indicator values (Dengler et al. 2023)
title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: EIVE 1.0 provides continuous ecological indicator values ~14,500 European vascular plant species. supersedes classic ordinal Ellenberg indicator values, expert-assigned integers 1-9 scale, statistically derived continuous scores based species co-occurrence patterns across thousands vegetation plots. Five niche axes covered: light, temperature, moisture, soil reaction (pH), nutrients. EIVE restricted European flora, species continents receive NA five columns. continuous values scale comparable original Ellenberg system (roughly 1-9) allow fractional positions. matters community-weighted mean (CWM) calculations: averaging ordinal Ellenberg values treats intervals categories equal (difference 3 4 7 8), guaranteed. EIVE’s continuous scale makes CWM calculations statistically cleaner. five output columns prefixed eive_ avoid collision columns sources: eive_light, eive_temperature, eive_moisture, eive_reaction, eive_nutrients. EIVE dataset licensed CC 4.0 published Zenodo. classified static taxify manifest version (1.0) fixed publication. reference Dengler et al. (2023), Vegetation Classification Survey 4:7-29.
code: grasses <- taxify(c(   "Arrhenatherum elatius", "Bromus erectus", "Festuca rubra",   "Dactylis glomerata", "Lolium perenne", "Poa pratensis" ))  grasses |> add_eive() #>          input_name eive_light eive_temperature eive_moisture eive_reaction eive_nutrients #> 1 Arrhenatherum ...       7.2              5.8           4.3           7.1            6.5 #> 2    Bromus erectus       7.6              5.5           3.1           7.8            3.2 #> ...

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: Diaz enrichment provides two key functional traits TRY database consortium: seed mass milligrams plant height metres. species-level means compiled thousands individual measurements across multiple primary sources. Coverage spans ~46,000 plant species globally, making one broader trait datasets available plants. Seed mass plant height sit two important axes global spectrum plant form function described Diaz et al. (2016, Nature 529:167-171). Seed mass captures offspring size / offspring number trade-(small-seeded species produce many propagules, large-seeded species invest fewer, better-provisioned offspring). Plant height captures competitive strategy axis (tall species intercept light invest structural tissue). Combining two traits EIVE LEDA columns produces reasonably complete functional characterization European temperate species. output columns seed_mass_mg plant_height_m. numeric (NA_real_ missing values). dataset licensed CC 3.0 distributed via TRY File Archive.
code: trees <- taxify(c(   "Quercus robur", "Fagus sylvatica", "Picea abies",   "Pinus sylvestris", "Acer pseudoplatanus" ))  trees |> add_diaz_traits() #>          input_name seed_mass_mg plant_height_m #> 1     Quercus robur      3200.0           25.0 #> 2  Fagus sylvatica      2200.0           30.0 #> 3      Picea abies        7.9           40.0 #> 4 Pinus sylvestris        6.5           25.0 #> 5 Acer pseudoplatanus   120.0           25.0

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: LEDA covers ~8,000 NW European plant species 10 trait columns spanning life form, dispersal, seed, leaf, clonality dimensions. column-rich simple enrichments, providing broad functional profile single call. full column set includes: raunkiaer_life_form: primary Raunkiaer life form (phanerophyte, chamaephyte, hemicryptophyte, geophyte, therophyte, helophyte, hydrophyte). raunkiaer_variable: 1 species assigned multiple life forms, 0 otherwise. dispersal_type: primary dispersal vector (anemochory, zoochory, hydrochory, autochory, barochory, dysochory). terminal_velocity_ms: seed terminal velocity m/s (species median). seed_mass_mg: seed mass mg (species median). canopy_height_m: canopy height metres (species median). leaf_mass_mg: leaf dry mass mg (species median). sla_mm2_mg: specific leaf area mm^2/mg (species median). clonal_growth: capable clonal growth (1 = yes, 0 = ). buoyancy: seed buoyancy classification. Raunkiaer life form column classifies plants perennating buds sit unfavorable season. Phanerophytes (trees, tall shrubs) hold buds 25 cm soil; chamaephytes (low shrubs, cushion plants) keep near surface. Hemicryptophytes, dominant group temperate grasslands, position buds right soil level. ground, geophytes store buds bulbs rhizomes, therophytes skip problem entirely surviving seeds. LEDA provides classification species level NW European flora, making one trait databases includes Raunkiaer assignments several thousand species. One column-name collision aware : LEDA’s seed_mass_mg Diaz enrichment’s seed_mass_mg share output column name. enrichments stacked pipe chain, second one run overwrite first. values may differ slightly LEDA reports species median measurements Diaz reports TRY consortium mean. keep , apply one enrichment, rename column, apply second. Alternatively, choose whichever source appropriate study: LEDA NW European analyses (regional measurements), Diaz global analyses (worldwide compilation).

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: conservation status enrichment enrichment spans taxonomic groups equally. Coverage includes ~166,000 species assessed IUCN Red List, representation across plants, vertebrates, invertebrates, fungi. single column added: conservation_status, containing standard IUCN category code. seven categories order increasing threat : LC (Least Concern): population stable, significant threats. NT (Near Threatened): close qualifying threatened category. VU (Vulnerable): facing high risk extinction wild. EN (Endangered): facing high risk extinction. CR (Critically Endangered): facing extremely high risk. EW (Extinct Wild): survives captivity cultivation. EX (Extinct): known living individuals. Species yet assessed IUCN receive NA. IUCN assessed nearly mammals, birds, amphibians, reptiles, fraction invertebrates, fungi, plants. plant-focused study, coverage rates likely lower (perhaps 10-30% species list) vertebrate study (90-100% coverage typical). enrichment also includes species assessed DD (Data Deficient), indicates IUCN examined species lacks sufficient data assign threat category. conservation status enrichment non-static: IUCN publishes updated assessments several times per year, taxify enrichment rebuilt new assessments become available. summary() output show version string (e.g., “2025.1”) exact assessment vintage can cited.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data

Dispersal ecology, macroecology, studies range expansion make heavy use index. migration column three possible values: "sedentary" (non- migratory), "partial" (populations migrate), "full" (obligate long-distance migrant). trophic_niche column uses categories like "Invertivore", "Omnivore", "Vertivore", "Frugivore", "Granivore", "Nectarivore", "Herbivore aquatic", others. title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: EltonTraits 1.0 covers birds mammals (~15,400 species total), making enrichment spans two vertebrate classes. adds 18 columns organized three groups: 10 diet composition percentages, 6 foraging stratum percentages, body mass, nocturnality. diet columns express percentage contribution food category species’ diet: invertebrates (diet_inv), endothermic vertebrates (diet_vend), ectothermic vertebrates (diet_vect), fish (diet_vfish), unknown vertebrates (diet_vunk), scavenging (diet_scav), fruit (diet_fruit), nectar (diet_nect), seeds nuts (diet_seed), plant material (diet_plantother). 10 diet percentages sum 100 species. foraging columns express vertical habitat structure species forages: water surface (foraging_water), ground (foraging_ground), understory (foraging_understory), mid high vegetation (foraging_midhigh), canopy (foraging_canopy), aerial (foraging_aerial). 6 percentages also sum 100. Common Swift (Apus apus) textbook example species extreme foraging diet axes: 100% invertebrate diet, 100% aerial foraging, reflecting life spent almost entirely wing. Barn Owl (Tyto alba) illustrates nocturnal flag: one species example set nocturnal = 1. elton_body_mass_g column provides body mass grams literature compilation. EltonTraits particularly valuable functional diversity analyses (computing Rao’s quadratic entropy functional richness using diet foraging traits axes), food web construction (using diet percentages parameterize trophic links), macroecological studies niche breadth. licensed CC0 published Figshare. reference Wilman et al. (2014), Ecology 95:2027.

title: Enriching results with trait and status data
text: PanTHERIA covers ~5,400 mammal species eight life-history ecological traits: adult body mass, maximum longevity, mean litter size, gestation length, weaning age, home range size, diet breadth, habitat breadth. remains -cited source mammalian life-history data ecological literature, despite published 2009. body mass column named pantheria_body_mass_g distinguish AVONET’s avonet_body_mass_g EltonTraits’ elton_body_mass_g. prefixing convention prevents column-name collisions stacking multiple enrichments result. title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: taxify() receives name find exact match, falls back fuzzy matching. Fuzzy matching computes string distance input name every candidate backbone, returns closest candidate whose distance falls threshold. backbone genus-blocked step: names sharing genus compared, keeps search fast even backbones millions rows. String distance purely mechanical measure. counts character-level edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions, optionally transpositions) required transform one string another. fuzzy match tells us two strings spelled similarly. tell us anything whether two names refer biological entity. Fuzzy matching catches typos, transliteration errors, OCR artefacts. resolve taxonomic disagreements, bridge gap common names Latin binomials. distinction matters interpretation. fuzzy match low distance (say 0.05) almost certainly corrects minor typo. fuzzy match high distance (say 0.18) might correct larger OCR error, might matched wrong species entirely. fuzzy_dist column output can tell apart. matching pipeline taxify runs strict sequence: name cleaning first, exact matching (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, Latin orthographic normalization, infraspecific--species fallback), fuzzy matching names survived exact passes without hit. Fuzzy matching never overrides exact match. cleaned input matches backbone name exactly, result stands regardless whether closer fuzzy candidate might exist different spelling. means fuzzy matching step operates genuinely misspelled garbled names.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: taxify supports three string distance algorithms, selected via fuzzy_method argument. three computed C level inside vectra’s fuzzy_join(), runs genus-blocked comparisons parallel via OpenMP.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Damerau-Levenshtein counts four edit operations, costing 1: Insertion: add character (Querus Quercus) Deletion: remove character (Quercuss Quercus) Substitution: replace one character another (Quarcus Quercus) Transposition: swap two adjacent characters (Qurecus Quercus) transposition operation distinguishes Damerau-Levenshtein plain Levenshtein. Transpositions among common typos hand-entered data, treating single edit (rather two: deletion plus insertion) produces tighter distances real-world errors. default good reason: handles common failure modes smallest distance penalty.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: Levenshtein supports three operations: insertion, deletion, substitution. transposition like Qurecus Quercus costs 2 edits (delete r, insert r right position) instead 1 edit Damerau-Levenshtein assign. practice means Levenshtein stricter Damerau-Levenshtein transposition-heavy errors identical everything else. threshold value reject candidates Damerau-Levenshtein accept. Levenshtein reasonable choice input data comes controlled source (database export, curated checklist) transpositions rare. OCR hand-typed data, Damerau-Levenshtein almost always better.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning

Jaro-Winkler operates 0--1 scale definition, fractional thresholds supported. Passing integer threshold (like fuzzy_threshold = 2) fuzzy_method = "jw" raises error immediately. Jaro-Winkler can useful short names (3-5 characters) single edit produces large normalized distance Damerau-Levenshtein, datasets errors concentrated epithet rather genus. general-purpose matching, Damerau-Levenshtein remains safer default.

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning
text: default threshold 0.2 means: normalized distance must exceed 0.2. Normalized distance defined : scales name length. 5-character name (Abies) gets 1 edit threshold 0.2, 1 / 5 = 0.2. 12-character name (Taraxacum -) gets 2 edits, 2 / 12 = 0.167 < 0.2 3 / 12 = 0.25 > 0.2. 20-character name gets 4 edits. concrete arithmetic representative names: “max edits” column floor(length * threshold). practice comparison uses floating-point ratio, floor, 9-character name 2 edits gives 2/9 = 0.222, exceeds 0.2 rejected.
code: normalized_distance = raw_edits / max(nchar(input), nchar(candidate))

title: Fuzzy matching: methods, thresholds, and tuning

title: Working with large species lists
text: Every backbone ships .vtr file: binary columnar format written vectra C11 engine. Unlike CSV TSV, .vtr format stores column contiguously disk lightweight compression. taxify never parses text query time. read.csv() step, string splitting, quote escaping. backbone already query-ready binary layout. Traditional taxonomic matching tools read CSV backbone every session start (WorldFlora takes 15-30 seconds parse WFO’s classification.txt), call remote API per name (taxize, subject rate limits network latency). taxify’s approach eliminates bottlenecks. one-time conversion Darwin Core CSV .vtr happens download time never needs repeated. resulting file typically 30-50% smaller original CSV columnar layout compresses string columns efficiently row-oriented text.

Case-insensitive match precomputed lowercased key. Latin orthographic normalization maps common epithet variants (e.g., -ii -, -anum -ana) canonical form. Infraspecific--species fallback strips variety/subspecies qualifiers matches binomial. Hybrid name normalization resolves nothospecies formatting differences (e.g., Salix x rubens vs. Salix xrubens). five passes use hash lookups. name matches pass 1 never tested passes 2-5. practice, pass 1 resolves 85-95% names clean input, passes 2-4 pick another 2-5%. total cost exact matching dominated hash lookups, O(1) per name regardless backbone size.

what: Worked example: exact vs. fuzzy matching
title: Working with large species lists
text: simplest performance lever fuzzy argument. input names clean (e.g., names curated database, existing taxonomic checklist, output previous taxify run), disabling fuzzy matching skips string-distance computation entirely. Consider list 10,000 plant names extracted herbarium database names already standard binomial form. time modes: exact-run order magnitude faster. exact pass matches names first try five-pass pipeline (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, Latin orthographic normalization, infraspecific fallback, hybrid normalization). Fuzzy matching picks remaining names minor misspellings, cost scales number unmatched names times average genus size backbone. ratio two modes depends input quality. list names extracted curated database (GBIF occurrence records, published checklist, previous taxify run), exact pass resolves 95-99% names fuzzy pass adds little. OCR-transcribed herbarium labels citizen science data frequent misspellings, exact pass might resolve 70-80% fuzzy pass becomes essential. practical two-pass pattern large lists: run exact-first, inspect unmatched names, decide whether fuzzy pass worth time. pattern especially useful 1-5% names need fuzzy matching. exact pass finishes seconds even 100,000 names, fuzzy pass operates much smaller subset. total wall time often less half single fuzzy = TRUE call take, fuzzy engine need allocate working memory build query tables names already matched exactly. One subtlety: second call taxify() re-materialize backbone. session cache first call still active, fuzzy-pass starts immediately string-distance computation. penalty splitting work two calls.
code: # Assume `species_list` is a character vector of 10,000 plant names  # Exact + fuzzy (default) t_fuzzy <- system.time(   result_fuzzy <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE) )  # Exact only t_exact <- system.time(   result_exact <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = FALSE) )  t_fuzzy["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>   18.4  t_exact["elapsed"] #> elapsed #>    2.1 # Pass 1: exact only result <- taxify(species_list, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = FALSE)  # How many names remain unmatched? n_unmatched <- sum(result$match_type == "none") message(n_unmatched, " names unmatched after exact pass")  # Pass 2: fuzzy only on the unmatched subset if (n_unmatched > 0) {   unmatched_names <- result$input_name[result$match_type == "none"]   fuzzy_result <- taxify(unmatched_names, backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE)    # Merge back   matched_rows <- fuzzy_result$match_type != "none"   idx <- match(fuzzy_result$input_name[matched_rows],                result$input_name)   result[idx, ] <- fuzzy_result[matched_rows, ] }

what: Worked example: batch processing a very large list
title: Working with large species lists
text: Lists 100,000 names common biodiversity informatics. national herbarium digitization project might produce 500,000 label transcriptions. metabarcoding pipeline might output 200,000 OTU labels. Processing single taxify() call works, splitting chunks gives two practical benefits: progress monitoring memory stability. taxify’s matching engine handles full vector internally, single taxify() call 500,000 names produce correct results. two practical issues arise scale. First, fuzzy-join working set grows input size: 500,000 names 10% unmatched means 50,000 fuzzy comparisons, scanning genus block. temporary .vtr files distance matrices many comparisons can spike memory several hundred MB. Second, R process interrupted mid-run (Ctrl+C, OOM kill, session timeout), entire result lost. Chunking 50,000-100,000 names keeps peak memory predictable provides natural restart points. backbone stays memory across chunks (session cache cleared calls), chunk first skips initialization overhead. fuzzy-join working set allocated freed per chunk. lists millions (e.g., processing occurrence records GBIF download), consider writing results disk chunk rather accumulating memory: pattern keeps R’s memory usage bounded single chunk regardless total list size. also makes workflow resumable: process dies chunk 47 60, can check .rds files exist restart chunk 48. Adding simple skip condition handles : One detail worth noting: chunk boundaries arbitrary affect matching quality. chunk matched independently backbone. name appears chunk 3 gets result appeared chunk 7 backbone deterministic matching logic stateless across calls. shared state chunks session cache (materialized backbone block), improves performance avoiding repeated initialization.
code: # 300,000 names from a herbarium digitization project all_names <- readLines("herbarium_names.txt") chunk_size <- 50000  # Split into chunks chunks <- split(all_names,                 ceiling(seq_along(all_names) / chunk_size))  # Process each chunk results <- lapply(seq_along(chunks), function(i) {   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d (%d names)...",                   i, length(chunks), length(chunks[[i]])))   taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo", fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) })  # Combine result <- do.call(rbind, results) nrow(result) #> [1] 300000 output_dir <- "results" dir.create(output_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)  for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d", i, length(chunks)))   res <- taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo",                 fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)   saveRDS(res, file.path(output_dir,                          sprintf("chunk_%04d.rds", i))) }  # Combine when needed all_files <- list.files(output_dir, pattern = "\\.rds$",                         full.names = TRUE) result <- do.call(rbind, lapply(all_files, readRDS)) for (i in seq_along(chunks)) {   out_file <- file.path(output_dir, sprintf("chunk_%04d.rds", i))   if (file.exists(out_file)) next   message(sprintf("Chunk %d/%d", i, length(chunks)))   res <- taxify(chunks[[i]], backend = "wfo",                 fuzzy = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)   saveRDS(res, out_file) }

text: default fuzzy threshold 0.2 (normalized Damerau-Levenshtein distance: edits divided maximum two name lengths). threshold 0.2 allows roughly one edit per five characters, catches single-character typos binomials typical length (15-25 characters). good default use cases. large lists noisy input (OCR, handwriting transcription), slightly higher threshold like 0.25 catches misspellings also increases false positives. clean input fuzzy matching serves safety net, lower threshold like 0.1 0.15 reduces risk incorrect matches without sacrificing much recall. threshold also affects performance. higher threshold means backbone entries pass distance filter, means candidate matches evaluate rank. difference modest inputs can noticeable large genera: threshold 0.2, query Astragalus (~3,000 WFO entries) might return 5 candidates; 0.3, might return 20. alternative mode uses integer thresholds. Setting fuzzy_threshold = 2L allows 2 raw edit operations regardless name length. useful long infraspecific names normalized threshold 0.2 might allow many edits. Results reproducible across sessions: query return different answers backbone updated runs. package’s core design made problems structural, incidental. Even someone maintains fork, API-first architecture remains fragile. WorldFlora still CRAN still works. reads WFO classification.txt file (~400 MB tab-separated download) performs string matching . limitations scope speed: supports WFO (plants), fuzzy matching beyond WFO.match.fuzzyjoin() provides via fuzzyjoin package, processes names iterating row--row backbone table. datasets hundred names, wait tolerable. datasets ten thousand , becomes bottleneck. taxify takes different approach. downloads Darwin Core backbone snapshots disk , converts compressed columnar format (.vtr files powered vectra C11 engine), runs matching offline. text: table lists taxize functions taxify replaces, along functions direct equivalent. key structural difference taxize returned results varied formats: get_tsn() returned character vector attributes, classification() returned nested list data.frames, synonyms() returned another nested list. taxify returns 16-column data.frame every call. what: Example 1: Basic name resolution (taxize vs. taxify)
title: Migrating from taxize and WorldFlora
text: typical taxize workflow resolved names gnr_resolve(), pulled classification synonyms separate calls. step hit different API. four steps makes network requests. GBIF API slow rate-limiting, whole pipeline stalls. results come back different shapes: resolved data.frame, gbif_ids character vector class attributes, class_list list data.frames (one per name), syn_list another list data.frames. taxify equivalent one function call. Name resolution, synonym resolution, classification handled together. output data.frame 16 columns one row per input name. nested lists, per-name API calls, internet connection required first run.
code: # --- taxize (no longer on CRAN) --- library(taxize)  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula",            "Panthera leo", "Salmo trutta")  # Step 1: resolve names via Global Names Resolver resolved <- gnr_resolve(names, best_match_only = TRUE)  # Step 2: get GBIF IDs (separate API call) gbif_ids <- get_gbifid(names)  # Step 3: get classification (yet another API call, per name) class_list <- classification(gbif_ids, db = "gbif")  # Step 4: check synonyms (another round of API calls) syn_list <- synonyms(gbif_ids, db = "gbif") # --- taxify --- library(taxify)  names <- c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris", "Betula pendula",            "Panthera leo", "Salmo trutta")  # One call: resolve, classify, and check synonyms result <- taxify(names, backend = "gbif")  # Everything is in the result data.frame: result$accepted_name result$family result$genus result$is_synonym result$taxon_id        # GBIF usage key

what: Example 2: WFO matching with fuzzy + synonyms (WorldFlora vs. taxify)
title: Migrating from taxize and WorldFlora
text: WorldFlora’s standard workflow loads classification file, runs exact matching, applies fuzzy matching unresolved names second pass. output wide data.frame WFO columns (scientificNameAuthorship, namePublishedIn, references, acceptedNameUsageID, ). user look taxonomicStatus tell accepted names synonyms, cross-reference acceptedNameUsageID taxonID find accepted name string. taxify folds exact matching, fuzzy matching, synonym resolution single call. Fuzzy matching default uses Damerau-Levenshtein distance genus blocking, faster accurate full-table string distance computation. Quercus pedonculata misspelling synonym. taxify handles one pass: fuzzy matcher corrects spelling Quercus pedunculata, synonym resolver maps Quercus robur (accepted name). WorldFlora requires user chain WFO.match.fuzzyjoin() manually trace acceptedNameUsageID.
code: # --- WorldFlora --- library(WorldFlora)  # User must download classification.txt manually (~400 MB) wfo_data <- read.delim("classification.txt")  # Exact match first names <- c("Quercus robur", "Quercus pedonculata",            "Pinus silvestris", "Rosa canina") exact <- WFO.match(names, WFO.data = wfo_data)  # Fuzzy match for unmatched (separate call, requires fuzzyjoin) fuzzy <- WFO.match.fuzzyjoin(names, WFO.data = wfo_data)  # Pick best match per name best <- WFO.one(fuzzy)  # Check synonym resolution best$taxonomicStatus best$acceptedNameUsageID # --- taxify --- library(taxify)  # No manual download needed (automatic on first use) names <- c("Quercus robur", "Quercus pedonculata",            "Pinus silvestris", "Rosa canina")  result <- taxify(names, backend = "wfo")  # Misspellings are caught by fuzzy matching: result[, c("input_name", "matched_name", "match_type", "fuzzy_dist")] #   input_name           matched_name        match_type fuzzy_dist # 1 Quercus robur        Quercus robur       exact              NA # 2 Quercus pedonculata  Quercus pedunculata fuzzy           0.053 # 3 Pinus silvestris     Pinus sylvestris    fuzzy           0.063 # 4 Rosa canina          Rosa canina         exact              NA  # Synonyms resolved automatically: result[, c("input_name", "is_synonym", "accepted_name")]

WorldFlora WFO-. taxify runs fallback chain unmatched names cascade next backbone automatically. fallback chain resolves plants WFO (nomenclatural coverage vascular plants strongest) passes animals, fungi, anything else WFO cover GBIF. user sees one data.frame. backend column records provenance. code: # --- taxify only (no taxize/WorldFlora equivalent) --- library(taxify)  # Mixed kingdom input: plants, animals, fungi names <- c(   "Quercus robur",         # plant (WFO primary)   "Panthera leo",          # animal (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Amanita muscaria",      # fungus (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Salmo trutta",          # fish (not in WFO, picked up by GBIF)   "Arabidopsis thaliana"   # plant (in both WFO and GBIF) )  # WFO first (best for plants), GBIF as fallback (all kingdoms) result <- taxify(names, backend = c("wfo", "gbif"))  # The backend column shows which database matched each name: result[, c("input_name", "backend", "family")] #   input_name            backend family # 1 Quercus robur         wfo     Fagaceae # 2 Panthera leo          gbif    Felidae # 3 Amanita muscaria      gbif    Amanitaceae # 4 Salmo trutta          gbif    Salmonidae # 5 Arabidopsis thaliana  wfo     Brassicaceae  # Layer on enrichments via the pipe: result |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_common_names(lang = "en")  # Or join custom data: my_traits <- data.frame(   species = c("Quercus robur", "Panthera leo"),   max_height_m = c(35, NA),   body_mass_kg = c(NA, 190) ) result |> add_data(my_traits, species_col = "species")

title: Migrating from taxize and WorldFlora
text: Online vs. offline. taxize sent HTTP requests every name. taxify downloads backbone files matches locally. initial download (typically 50–300 MB depending backbone), internet connection needed. Queries local backbone run milliseconds per name. Single database vs. multi-backend. taxize query multiple databases, separate function call different return type. WorldFlora supported WFO. taxify supports nine backbones single function, optional fallback chains cascade unmatched names automatically. Output format. taxize returned different types depending function: character vectors (get_tsn()), lists data.frames (classification()), nested lists (synonyms()). title: Getting started with taxify
text: taxify classifies every match one five categories. category reflects different level confidence result. exact: cleaned input matches backbone entry character character. fastest path reliable. practice, majority well-formatted species names fall category. exact_ci: input matches case folding Latin orthographic normalization. edit distance involved. category catches two distinct classes mismatch: pure capitalization differences, Latin spelling alternations. name “quercus robur” (lowercase) match “Quercus robur” character--character, match case folding. match type exact_ci fuzzy_dist remains NA edit distance algorithm needed. fuzzy: input match exactly falls within allowed edit distance backbone entry. default threshold 0.2 (normalized Damerau-Levenshtein), allows roughly one edit per five characters. Fuzzy matching genus-blocked: “Quercus robor” compared Quercus entries, entire backbone. typo “robor” (missing “u”) corrected “Quercus robur” normalized edit distance 0.07. fuzzy_dist column always holds normalized distance, values comparable across names different lengths. out_of_scope: genus recognized genus register covered requested backbone. Submitting “Panthera leo” WFO backbone (plants ) produces classification, taxify knows Panthera real genus belongs different backbone. life_form column (populated genus register) shows “animal”, explains name scope plant-backbone. summary method uses information suggest alternative backends. none: match found genus either unknown also covered requested backend. means name genuinely absent backbone, merely scoped different one. “Fakegenus fakus” recognized backbone, receives none. genus register entry “Fakegenus”, alternative backend suggest. real-world datasets, none typically indicates garbled name, common name converted Latin, organism described publication predates backbone’s coverage. five categories together give complete picture match quality. quick diagnostic pass output might look like : Fuzzy matching controlled three arguments. fuzzy = FALSE disables entirely. fuzzy_threshold sets maximum normalized distance (default 0.2) , >= 1, raw edit count (e.g., fuzzy_threshold = 2L allows 2 edits regardless name length). fuzzy_method selects algorithm: "dl" (Damerau-Levenshtein, default), "levenshtein", "jw" (Jaro-Winkler).

text: taxify detects hybrid markers input names (multiplication sign, letter x genus epithet, two binomials) sets is_hybrid = TRUE output. add_hybrid_info() goes , parsing hybrid formulas extract parent names classify hybrid type. Three hybrid types recognized. “nothospecies” named hybrid species (multiplication sign appears genus epithet). “formula” hybrid cross written “x B”, parser expands abbreviated genera (“Q.” “Q. petraea” expanded “Quercus” based first parent). “nothogenus” hybrid genus (multiplication sign precedes genus name). formulas, extracted parent names full binomials can submitted taxify() resolution. common workflow hybrid-heavy datasets (e.g., ornamental horticulture records) run taxify() full list, pipe add_hybrid_info(), finally re-run taxify() extracted parent names obtain full taxonomic resolution. hybrid detection runs raw input cleaning, correctly handles Unicode multiplication sign ASCII “x” notation.

title: Getting started with taxify
text: close end--end worked example. input realistic list 22 species names hypothetical European biodiversity survey. includes six clean accepted plant names, three historical synonyms (Pinus abies, Quercus pedunculata, Picea excelsa), two typos (Quercus robor, Fagus sylvatyca), four messy field annotations (qualifier, trailing authorship, infraspecific rank, excess whitespace), one hybrid, four animal names belong plant backbone, two entirely fictitious names. match WFO COL, inspect summary, layer three enrichments. 22 names total. match WFO first (best plants), COL fallback animal names. WFO handled plant names (exact, fuzzy, synonym, ). 4 animal names match WFO forwarded COL, resolved four. Let us look summary first. Eighteen 22 names matched. two unknowns “Notareal plantus” “Randomus specius”, exist backbone. animal names scope time COL covers . Now layer three enrichments: conservation status, woodiness, common names. summary now reflects enrichment coverage. Conservation status matched 12 22 names, woodiness matched 14, common names matched 16. numbers make sense. Conservation status gaps every species assessed IUCN; common European trees assessed (mostly LC), less prominent species may . Woodiness covers 48,000 species Zanne et al. dataset, temperate trees included. Common names widest coverage GBIF vernacular names dataset large, though still misses species less commonly documented languages. can now pull columns care downstream analysis. result plain data.frame, standard R subsetting, dplyr verbs, data.table operations work without special handling. diagnostic queries round workflow. patterns come nearly every biodiversity data cleaning session. Three synonyms submitted. “Pinus abies” “Picea excelsa” resolve accepted species, Picea abies, accepted ID. “Quercus pedunculata” resolves Quercus robur. synonyms share accepted IDs rows result, enrichment columns carry trait values accepted-name counterparts. common pattern biodiversity databases: physical species appears different names different records, synonym resolution step collapses variants trait lookups species counts correct. Without step, species count overcount Picea abies (listing three times three different names) trait join miss synonym rows entirely. typos corrected low edit distances. “y” “sylvatyca” caught Damerau-Levenshtein, missing “u” “robor” caught way. one species survey carries threatened IUCN status. rest either Least Concern yet assessed. European forest plot unsurprising. tropical marine dataset likely show threatened species. conservation status enrichment cross-kingdom, works animal names well (Panthera leo VU globally). matched plants survey woody, makes sense European forest plot. 8 NAs correspond 4 animal names (woodiness plant-enrichment), 2 unknown names, 2 plants happen fall outside Zanne et al. dataset coverage. entire pipeline ran offline initial backbone download. API rate limits, network dependency analysis, backbone_version column output ensures full reproducibility. closing notes performance. taxify uses vectra’s columnar engine backbone queries. Exact matching index-accelerated (hash indexes name genus columns), even GBIF backbone (7 million rows) batch 10,000 names typically completes seconds. Fuzzy matching slower involves string distance computation, genus-blocked: input name compared backbone entries genus, reduces search space several orders magnitude. large batches (100,000+ names), main bottleneck fuzzy matching names misspelled genera, genus blocking help. case, consider running first pass fuzzy = FALSE pick easy matches, re-running unmatched names fuzzy enabled. enrichment joins also fast. add_*() call performs single vectra inner join accepted_name, O(n) size taxify result. Stacking multiple enrichments pipe adds columns incrementally without re-reading backbone.

Sorbus aucuparia", "Acer pseudoplatanus L.",   "Pinus sylvestris var. hamata", "  Tilia   cordata  ",   "Quercus x rosacea",   "Panthera leo", "Salmo trutta", "Cervus elaphus", "Parus major",   "Notareal plantus", "Randomus specius" ) result <- taxify(survey_names, backend = c("wfo", "col")) #> Matching 22 names against 2 backends: wfo -> col #>   [wfo] Matching 22 names... #>   [wfo] Fuzzy matching 6 unmatched... #>   [col] Matching 4 remaining names... summary(result) #> -- taxify results ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   backend: WFO + COL  |  22 names submitted #> #>   matched        18  (exact: 13, case-insensitive: 0, fuzzy: 2) #>   out of scope    0 #>   unmatched       2  (taxon_group: unknown: 2) #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   taxon groups: vascular plant: 16  animal: 4  unknown: 2 result <- result |>   add_conservation_status() |>   add_woodiness() |>   add_common_names() summary(result) #> -- taxify results ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   backend: WFO + COL  |  22 names submitted #> #>   matched        18  (exact: 13, case-insensitive: 0, fuzzy: 2) #>   out of scope    0 #>   unmatched       2  (taxon_group: unknown: 2) #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   taxon groups: vascular plant: 16  animal: 4  unknown: 2 #> #>   enrichments: #>     conservation_status  (IUCN Red List 2024.12)      -- 12 of 22 matched #>     woodiness            (Zanne et al. 2014 2024.12)  -- 14 of 22 matched #>     common_names         (GBIF vernacular names 2024.12) -- 16 of 22 matched analysis <- result[, c("input_name", "accepted_name", "family",                         "match_type", "is_synonym", "backend",                         "conservation_status", "woodiness",                         "common_name")] # Which names were synonyms? result[result$is_synonym == TRUE,        c("input_name", "accepted_name", "accepted_id")] #>            input_name  accepted_name       accepted_id #> 7         Pinus abies    Picea abies  wfo-0000471692 #> 8  Quercus pedunculata Quercus robur  wfo-0000306015 #> 9     Picea excelsa      Picea abies  wfo-0000471692 # Which names needed fuzzy correction? result[result$match_type == "fuzzy",        c("input_name", "accepted_name", "fuzzy_dist")] #>        input_name  accepted_name fuzzy_dist #> 10  Quercus robor  Quercus robur 0.07142857 #> 11 Fagus sylvatyca Fagus sylvatica 0.06666667 # Threatened species in the survey result[!is.na(result$conservation_status) &        result$conservation_status %in% c("VU", "EN", "CR"),        c("accepted_name", "conservation_status", "common_name")] #>     accepted_name conservation_status common_name #> 17  Panthera leo                  VU        Lion # Woody vs. herbaceous breakdown table(result$woodiness, useNA = "ifany") #> herbaceous      woody       <NA> #>          0         14          8

title: taxify — Claude Code Context
text: Seven backends implemented: WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI, OTT, WoRMS. Adding backend requires: 1. Constructor function (e.g., col_backend()) 2. S3 methods: taxify_download, taxify_load, match_exact, match_fuzzy 3. Register resolve_backend() switch Build pipeline separation: taxify package contains backend R code (S3 methods, matching logic, build--source fallback). Pre-built .vtr files built separate taxify-backbones repo (C:\Users\Gilles Colling\Documents\dev\taxify-backbones), shared normalize/precompute/build pipeline CI workflows.

title: taxify — Claude Code Context
text: WFO: Single classification.txt TSV. scientificName canonical (authorship). Column genus. COL: Single Taxon.tsv dwc:/col: prefixed headers (stripped read). scientificName includes authorship → canonicalName computed stripping authorship. Column genericName (genus). Status values originally lowercase (uppercased conversion). SpeciesProfile.tsv stored separate .vtr extinct/marine info. GBIF: simple.txt.gz header row (30 positional columns), \N NULLs. canonical_name already exists. Column genus_or_above. family text column — family_key FK, denormalized conversion via self-join. Synonyms use parent_key accepted ID (acceptedNameUsageID). Status values like HOMOTYPIC_SYNONYM, HETEROTYPIC_SYNONYM mapped standard SYNONYM. ITIS: SQLite dump itis.gov. Uses unified backbone schema (canonical_name, taxon_id, etc.). Relational: taxonomic_units + synonym_links + taxon_unit_types. Family/genus resolved via parent_tsn hierarchy walk. name_usage (valid/accepted → ACCEPTED, invalid/accepted → SYNONYM). Column genus (resolved). Requires RSQLite (Suggests) build--source; pre-built .vtr preferred. NCBI: taxdump.tar.gz pipe-delimited .dmp files (names.dmp, nodes.dmp). Unified schema. Synonyms alternative name rows tax_id, emitted separate rows synthetic IDs (tax_id_syn_N). Family/genus via hierarchy walk. authorship data. Aggressive noise filtering (environmental samples, unclassified, metagenomes). OTT: OTT taxonomy archive (taxonomy.tsv + synonyms.tsv, pipe-delimited). Unified schema. Synthetic taxonomy combining NCBI, GBIF, WoRMS, IRMNG. Status derived flags column. Family/genus via hierarchy walk. Cross-references source databases via sourceinfo column. WoRMS: DwC-GBIF ChecklistBank (dataset 1010). Unified schema. Marine-focused. taxonID may LSID (stripped numeric AphiaID). Status uses accepted/unaccepted (mapped standard). Classification denormalized (hierarchy walk). SpeciesProfile.tsv habitat flags.

title: taxify — Plan
text: fundamental interface question: one---time vs batch. WorldFlora approach: - WFO.one(name, WFO.data) — single name, returns full match details - WFO.match(names, WFO.data) — vectorized, returns data.frame taxify : Things decide: - taxify() always return one row per input name? can return multiple candidates? - multiple candidates, user pick? Interactive? Score-based? Top-1 alternatives list column? - fuzzy matching opt-(fuzzy = TRUE) default? - user specify fallback chain? backends = c("wfo", "col") tries WFO first, COL unmatched?
code: # Option A: single function, vectorized taxify(names, backends = "wfo", ...)  # Option B: explicit one vs many taxify_one(name, backends = "wfo", ...)    # detailed single match taxify(names, backends = "wfo", ...)        # batch, returns data.frame  # Option C: match then resolve as separate steps matches <- taxify_match(names, backends = "wfo", ...) resolved <- taxify_resolve(matches)

title: taxify — Plan
text: backend = one module implements interface. Adding new backend O(1) effort. Query engine: vectra (dependency, gcol33/vectra) backbone querying goes vectra’s columnar engine: - tbl_csv() / tbl() stream Darwin Core files larger RAM - Exact matching via inner_join() canonical name (hash join, streaming) - Fuzzy matching via levenshtein() / levenshtein_norm() C-level expressions (implemented vectra — stays engine, collect--R roundtrip) - Candidate pre-filtering genus fuzzy step (selection vectors, zero-copy) - Cleaning pipeline: tolower(), trimws(), gsub() vectra’s C engine means: - data.table dependency — vectra handles heavy lifting - manual batch splitting — vectra streams row groups automatically - Fuzzy matching 40k names genus-filtered backbone fast Levenshtein runs C pre-filtered candidate set Backbone storage: .vtr (vectra native format) first download. First taxify_download() fetches Darwin Core CSV, converts .vtr fast repeated queries. Backends support: - WFO — World Flora Online (plants, offline Darwin Core snapshot Zenodo) - COL — Catalogue Life (kingdoms, annual checklist export) - GBIF — GBIF backbone taxonomy (kingdoms, downloadable — note: deprecated favor COL) backend needs: - download() — fetch Darwin Core archive convert .vtr - load() — open via vectra::tbl() (lazy, RAM cost) - match() — exact join + fuzzy via vectra expressions - resolve() — synonym → accepted name via join acceptedNameUsageID

title: taxify — Plan
text: cross-cutting concern touches parsing, matching, output. x problem: - × (Unicode multiplication sign) — unambiguous hybrid marker - x (lowercase letter x) — ambiguous. hybrid, part name, typo. - Need strategy: trust × always, trust x specific positions (genus epithet, two binomials), confirm backbone? Hybrid types: 1. Named hybrids / nothogenera: × Festulolium — named hybrid genus, treated single taxon backbones 2. Nothospecies: Quercus × hispanica — named hybrid species 3. Hybrid formulas: Quercus pyrenaica × Q. petraea — two parents, may exist named entity backbone 4. Ambiguous: Salix x fragilis — hybrid bad formatting? Strategy options: - ) Parse hybrid notation, set is_hybrid = TRUE, try match full hybrid name backbone. exists, done. , split match parents separately. - B) Always try match name -first. parse hybrid initial match fails. - C) Parse hybrids upfront, match hybrid name parents, return whichever gives best match.

what: taxify: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots — taxify-package
title: taxify: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots — taxify-package
text: Match taxonomic names locally stored Darwin Core backbone databases (WFO, COL, GBIF, ITIS, NCBI Taxonomy, Open Tree Life, WoRMS, Species Fungorum, AlgaeBase). Provides offline fuzzy exact matching synonym resolution, hybrid name detection, unified output schema across backends. heavy computation runs 'vectra' C11 columnar engine.

title: taxify: Offline Taxonomic Name Matching Against Local Darwin Core Snapshots — taxify-package
text: Maintainer: Gilles Colling gilles.colling051@gmail.com (ORCID) [copyright holder]

what: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
text: Matches vector taxonomic names locally stored Darwin Core backbone databases. Returns data.frame one row per input name containing matched name, accepted name, taxonomic hierarchy, match quality information.

title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
code: taxify(   x,   backend = "wfo",   fuzzy = TRUE,   fuzzy_threshold = 0.2,   fuzzy_method = c("dl", "levenshtein", "jw"),   verbose = TRUE )

title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
text: x Character vector taxonomic names. backend Character vector backend names (e.g., "wfo", "col", "gbif") single taxify_backend object. multiple backends given, tried order fallback chain. Default "wfo". fuzzy Logical. Enable fuzzy matching names fail exact match. Default TRUE. fuzzy_threshold Numeric. Maximum allowed distance fuzzy matches. Two modes depending value: Fractional (0 < fuzzy_threshold < 1): normalized distance (edits / max name length). Default 0.2 ≈ 1 edit per 5 characters. Integer (fuzzy_threshold >= 1): maximum raw edit count, e.g. fuzzy_threshold = 2L allows 2 insertions/deletions/substitutions regardless name length. supported fuzzy_method = "jw". fuzzy_method Character. One "dl" (Damerau-Levenshtein, default), "levenshtein", "jw" (Jaro-Winkler). verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default TRUE.

title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
text: data.frame one row per input name 16 columns: input_name original name provided. matched_name Full name backbone matched. accepted_name Resolved accepted name (equals matched_name synonym). taxon_id Backend-specific ID matched name. accepted_id ID accepted name. rank Taxonomic rank (species, subspecies, genus, etc.). family Family name. genus Genus name. epithet Specific epithet. authorship Authorship matched name. is_synonym Logical. match synonym? is_hybrid Logical. hybrid marker detected input? match_type One "exact", "exact_ci", "fuzzy", "none". fuzzy_dist Normalized string distance (0–1), NA exact. backend backend used (e.g., "wfo", "col", "gbif"). backbone_version Backend name, version, download date (e.g., "wfo:2024-12 (2026-04-01)"). Useful reproducibility.

title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
text: multiple backends specified, names matched backend order. Names matched earlier backend re-matched later ones (fallback chain).

title: Match taxonomic names against local backbone databases — taxify
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Match a few names (downloads WFO backbone on first use) taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Pinus sylvestris"))  # Disable fuzzy matching taxify("Quercus robus", fuzzy = FALSE)  # Fallback chain: try WFO first, then COL for unmatched taxify(c("Quercus robur", "Panthera leo"),        backend = c("wfo", "col")) } # }
